retrieved_docs.title: Dust Mite Allergy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Microarrays. It can be used to detect multiple antigens on a single slide. It includes single-plex (ImmunoCAP) and multiplex (ImmunoCAP ISAC) assays. One commercially available microarray is ImmunoCAP immunosorbent allergen chip (ISAC) that is used to define an individual's complete allergen sensitivity profile. A better available microarray is a MeDALL chip that can be used to monitor IgE and IgG reactivity profiles toward more than 170 allergens, but this is still under evaluation. These assays are expensive and available in large hospitals, thus cannot be used to detect dust mite allergy sensitization at the primary point of care. [22]

A big advantage to IgE serum results is there is no subjectivity, potential for operator error, and the results can be compared and reviewed later. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Protein Intolerance -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Radioallergosorbent test (RAST): This test detects allergen-specific Ig-E antibodies via radioimmunoassay. It is useful in determining the causes of allergies and is frequently positive in immediate GI hypersensitivity.

retrieved_docs.title: Dust Mite Allergy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Basophil activation test (BAT). It is a quantitative test that detects the activation markers on the basophil surface in whole blood. This test can be performed in patients who are already taking antihistamines. It also demonstrates a functional response. However, inconsistent results may be present because of the different varieties of commercial testing kits and varied protocols used. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Protein Intolerance -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Skin prick test: This test screens for IgE-mediated food protein allergies. Although a positive test result may show the presence of IgE antibodies, it has a low predictive value, whereas a negative test result has high predictive accuracy.

retrieved_docs.title: Dust Mite Allergy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Radio allergen sorbent test (RAST). It is an in vitro test for the detection of IgE levels. The serum IgE of the patient bound to the allergen is immobilized on a solid substrate, and then these are detected using radiolabelled anti-IgE antibodies. Its use has become limited due to the availability of better techniques like ELISA. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Allergy Testing -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Serum IgE Testing
retrieved_docs.content: High levels of IgE do not necessarily correlate with the severity of the reaction. The serum test results need to be analyzed with the patient’s history and clinical exam context and cannot be strictly diagnosed with just the IgE levels. False positives may occur. It is possible since each allergen is tested specifically that the correct allergen was not tested for. [5] High levels may also correlate with more chronic allergic diseases vs. acute. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Exercise-Induced Bronchoconstriction -- Evaluation -- Allergy Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should consider skin allergen testing or a radioallergosorbent test to identify specific allergens contributing to symptoms. Identifying these triggers can guide patients in avoiding causative antigens and inform decisions about initiating allergen-specific immunotherapy if indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.content: Aurothioglucose and sodium aurothiomalate are both classified within the structural class of allergens, indicating that each of these gold‑containing compounds has the potential to provoke allergic reactions. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 578
retrieved_docs.content: Gold preparations include the compounds aurothioglucose and aurothiomalate sodium, both of which are classified as members of the gold preparation group. These same compounds also define structural classes of allergens, indicating that aurothioglucose and aurothiomalate sodium can act as allergenic substances. Recognizing that these gold‑containing agents serve both therapeutic roles as gold preparations and potential roles as allergen structural classes is important for clinicians to anticipate and manage possible hypersensitivity reactions in patients receiving these medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2779
retrieved_docs.content: Atrakurium, imatinib mesylát, and etodolac are each contraindicated in the presence of فرط الحساسية الدوائية (drug hypersensitivity). This shared contraindication underscores the necessity of evaluating a patient’s allergy history before prescribing any of these medications, as exposure in individuals with drug hypersensitivity could precipitate severe allergic reactions. Proper assessment and avoidance of these agents in susceptible patients are essential for safe and effective patient care.
